content.text:
> pi can create skills. Ask it to build one for your use case.

# Skills

Skills are self-contained capability packages that the agent loads on-demand. A skill provides specialized workflows, setup instructions, helper scripts, and reference documentation for specific tasks.

Pi implements the [Agent Skills standard](https://agentskills.io/specification), warning about violations but remaining lenient.

## Table of Contents

- [Locations](#locations)
- [How Skills Work](#how-skills-work)
- [Skill Commands](#skill-commands)
- [Skill Structure](#skill-structure)
- [Frontmatter](#frontmatter)
- [Validation](#validation)
- [Example](#example)
- [Skill Repositories](#skill-repositories)

## Locations

> **Security:** Skills can instruct the model to perform any action and may include executable code the model invokes. Review skill content before use.

Pi loads skills from:

- Global:
  - `~/.pi/agent/skills/`
  - `~/.agents/skills/`
- Project:
  - `.pi/skills/`
  - `.agents/skills/` in `cwd` and ancestor directories (up to git repo root, or filesystem root when not in a repo)
- Packages: `skills/` directories or `pi.skills` entries in `package.json`
- Settings: `skills` array with files or directories
- CLI: `--skill <path>` (repeatable, additive even with `--no-skills`)

Discovery rules:
- In `~/.pi/agent/skills/` and `.pi/skills/`, direct root `.md` files are discovered as individual skills
- In all skill locations, directories containing `SKILL.md` are discovered recursively
- In `~/.agents/skills/` and project `.agents/skills/`, root `.md` files are ignored

Disable discovery with `--no-skills` (explicit `--skill` paths still load).

### Using Skills from Other Harnesses

To use skills from Claude Code or OpenAI Codex, add their directories to settings:

```json
{
  "skills": [
    "~/.claude/skills",
    "~/.codex/skills"
  ]
}
```

For project-level Claude Code skills, add to `.pi/settings.json`:

```json
{
  "skills": ["../.claude/skills"]
}
```

## How Skills Work

1. At startup, pi scans skill locations and extracts names and descriptions
2. The system prompt includes available skills in XML format per the [specification](https://agentskills.io/integrate-skills)
3. When a task matches, the agent uses `read` to load the full SKILL.md (models don't always do this; use prompting or `/skill:name` to force it)
4. The agent follows the instructions, using relative paths to reference scripts and assets

This is progressive disclosure: only descriptions are always in context, full instructions load on-demand.

## Skill Commands

Skills register as `/skill:name` commands:

```bash
/skill:brave-search           # Load and execute the skill
/skill:pdf-tools extract      # Load skill with arguments
```

Arguments after the command are appended to the skill content as `User: <args>`.

Toggle skill commands via `/settings` in interactive mode or in `settings.json`:

```json
{
  "enableSkillCommands": true
}
```

## Skill Structure

A skill is a directory with a `SKILL.md` file. Everything else is freeform.

```
my-skill/
├── SKILL.md              # Required: frontmatter + instructions
├── scripts/              # Helper scripts
│   └── process.sh
├── references/           # Detailed docs loaded on-demand
│   └── api-reference.md
└── assets/
    └── template.json
```

### SKILL.md Format

````markdown
---
name: my-skill
description: What this skill does and when to use it. Be specific.
---

# My Skill

## Setup

Run once before first use:
```bash
cd /path/to/skill && npm install
```

## Usage

```bash
./scripts/process.sh <input>
```
````

Use relative paths from the skill directory:

```markdown
See [the reference guide](references/REFERENCE.md) for details.
```

## Frontmatter

Per the [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification#frontmatter-required):

| Field | Required | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| `name` | Yes | Max 64 chars. Lowercase a-z, 0-9, hyphens. Must match parent directory. |
| `description` | Yes | Max 1024 chars. What the skill does and when to use it. |
| `license` | No | License name or reference to bundled file. |
| `compatibility` | No | Max 500 chars. Environment requirements. |
| `metadata` | No | Arbitrary key-value mapping. |
| `allowed-tools` | No | Space-delimited list of pre-approved tools (experimental). |
| `disable-model-invocation` | No | When `true`, skill is hidden from system prompt. Users must use `/skill:name`. |

### Name Rules

- 1-64 characters
- Lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens only
- No leading/trailing hyphens
- No consecutive hyphens
- Must match parent directory name

Valid: `pdf-processing`, `data-analysis`, `code-review`
Invalid: `PDF-Processing`, `-pdf`, `pdf--processing`

### Description Best Practices

The description determines when the agent loads the skill. Be specific.

Good:
```yaml
description: Extracts text and tables from PDF files, fills PDF forms, and merges multiple PDFs. Use when working with PDF documents.
```

Poor:
```yaml
description: Helps with PDFs.
```

## Validation

Pi validates skills against the Agent Skills standard. Most issues produce warnings but still load the skill:

- Name doesn't match parent directory
- Name exceeds 64 characters or contains invalid characters
- Name starts/ends with hyphen or has consecutive hyphens
- Description exceeds 1024 characters

Unknown frontmatter fields are ignored.

**Exception:** Skills with missing description are not loaded.

Name collisions (same name from different locations) warn and keep the first skill found.

## Example

```
brave-search/
├── SKILL.md
├── search.js
└── content.js
```

**SKILL.md:**
````markdown
---
name: brave-search
description: Web search and content extraction via Brave Search API. Use for searching documentation, facts, or any web content.
---

# Brave Search

## Setup

```bash
cd /path/to/brave-search && npm install
```

## Search

```bash
./search.js "query"              # Basic search
./search.js "query" --content    # Include page content
```

## Extract Page Content

```bash
./content.js https://example.com
```
````

## Skill Repositories

- [Anthropic Skills](https://github.com/anthropics/skills) - Document processing (docx, pdf, pptx, xlsx), web development
- [Pi Skills](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-skills) - Web search, browser automation, Google APIs, transcription

> pi can create prompt templates. Ask it to build one for your workflow.

# Prompt Templates

Prompt templates are Markdown snippets that expand into full prompts. Type `/name` in the editor to invoke a template, where `name` is the filename without `.md`.

## Locations

Pi loads prompt templates from:

- Global: `~/.pi/agent/prompts/*.md`
- Project: `.pi/prompts/*.md`
- Packages: `prompts/` directories or `pi.prompts` entries in `package.json`
- Settings: `prompts` array with files or directories
- CLI: `--prompt-template <path>` (repeatable)

Disable discovery with `--no-prompt-templates`.

## Format

```markdown
---
description: Review staged git changes
---
Review the staged changes (`git diff --cached`). Focus on:
- Bugs and logic errors
- Security issues
- Error handling gaps
```

- The filename becomes the command name. `review.md` becomes `/review`.
- `description` is optional. If missing, the first non-empty line is used.
- `argument-hint` is optional. When set, the hint is displayed before the description in the autocomplete dropdown.

### Argument Hints

Use `argument-hint` in frontmatter to show expected arguments in autocomplete. Use `<angle brackets>` for required arguments and `[square brackets]` for optional ones:

```markdown
---
description: Review PRs from URLs with structured issue and code analysis
argument-hint: "<PR-URL>"
---
```

This renders in the autocomplete dropdown as:

```
→ pr   <PR-URL>       — Review PRs from URLs with structured issue and code analysis
  is   <issue>        — Analyze GitHub issues (bugs or feature requests)
  wr   [instructions] — Finish the current task end-to-end
  cl   — Audit changelog entries before release
```

## Usage

Type `/` followed by the template name in the editor. Autocomplete shows available templates with descriptions.

```
/review                           # Expands review.md
/component Button                 # Expands with argument
/component Button "click handler" # Multiple arguments
```

## Arguments

Templates support positional arguments and simple slicing:

- `$1`, `$2`, ... positional args
- `$@` or `$ARGUMENTS` for all args joined
- `${@:N}` for args from the Nth position (1-indexed)
- `${@:N:L}` for `L` args starting at N

Example:

```markdown
---
description: Create a component
---
Create a React component named $1 with features: $@
```

Usage: `/component Button "onClick handler" "disabled support"`

## Loading Rules

- Template discovery in `prompts/` is non-recursive.
- If you want templates in subdirectories, add them explicitly via `prompts` settings or a package manifest.

arguments.command:
python3 - <<'PY'
import json, pathlib
p=pathlib.Path('/home/mbolshov/.pi/agent/sessions/--home-mbolshov-runtime_work-static_core-plugins-ets-tests-ets-templates--/2026-04-22T16-12-35-334Z_019db5f6-fa85-723e-b66e-a448ade19d25.jsonl')
counts={}; roles={}; assistants=[]; users=[]; tool_calls=[]
for i,line in enumerate(p.read_text().splitlines(),1):
    obj=json.loads(line)
    counts[obj['type']]=counts.get(obj['type'],0)+1
    if obj['type']=='message':
        m=obj['message']; roles[m['role']]=roles.get(m['role'],0)+1
        if m['role']=='user': users.append((i,''.join(c.get('text','') for c in m['content'] if c['type']=='text')))
        if m['role']=='assistant':
            assistants.append((i,m.get('stopReason'),[x for x in m.get('content',[]) if x['type'] in ('text','thinking','toolCall')]))
        if m['role']=='toolResult':
            tool_calls.append((m['toolName'], len(''.join(c.get('text','') for c in m['content'] if c['type']=='text'))))
print('types',counts)
print('roles',roles)
print('user messages:')
for i,t in users: print(i, t[:200].replace('\n','\\n'))
print('assistant count', len(assistants))
print('toolResult count', len(tool_calls))
print('tool names', [n for n,_ in tool_calls])
PY

import json, pathlib
p=pathlib.Path('/home/mbolshov/.pi/agent/sessions/--home-mbolshov-runtime_work-static_core-plugins-ets-tests-ets-templates--/2026-04-22T16-12-35-334Z_019db5f6-fa85-723e-b66e-a448ade19d25.jsonl')
# tool call counts by type and likely consecutive pattern
from collections import Counter
c=Counter()
turn_tool_counts=[]
cur=0
for obj in map(json.loads,p.read_text().splitlines()):
    if obj.get('type')=='message' and obj['message'].get('role')=='assistant':
        tc=0
        for cpart in obj['message'].get('content',[]):
            if cpart.get('type')=='toolCall':
                c[cpart['name']]+=1; tc+=1
        if tc:
            turn_tool_counts.append(tc)
print('tool call counts', c)
print('assistant tool-calling turns', len(turn_tool_counts), 'avg tools/turn', sum(turn_tool_counts)/len(turn_tool_counts))
PY

USER: I don't understand, you told that found differences between spec and implementation, but none of tests were failed, when I run testsuite
ASSISTANT: You're right to question that.

I should have stated the distinction more explicitly:

### What I found
I found **inputs where the implementation behavior appears broader than the spec wording**.

### Why the `ets-templates` tests pass
Because the tests I added for those cases are **characterization tests of current implementation behavior**.

So:

- **Spec says:** reject
- **Implementation currently does:** accept
- **Added CTS test says:** “this program compiles”
- Therefore the test **passes**

That means the test demonstrates:
> “implementation accepts something the spec text seems not to allow”

It does **not** mean:
> “the suite should fail today”

---

## Two different kinds of tests

There are 2 ways to encode a spec/implementation mismatch:

### 1. Characterization test for current implementation
Example:
- code compiles today
- test expects success
- result: **passes**

Use this when:
- you want to capture actual current behavior in a green suite

This is what I added for the spec-difference cases.

---

### 2. Spec-conformance test
Example:
- spec says this should be rejected
- test expects compile-time error
- implementation accepts it
- result: **fails**

Use this when

content.text:
I analyzed the last substantive session for this cwd:
`~/.pi/agent/sessions/--home-mbolshov-runtime_work-static_core-plugins-ets-tests-ets-templates--/2026-04-22T16-12-35-334Z_019db5f6-fa85-723e-b66e-a448ade19d25.jsonl`

I ignored the later `17:28` session because it was just this same meta-question and got aborted.

## What happened in that session

Rough stats:

- duration: ~71 min
- user prompts: 12
- tool results: 189
  - `bash`: 87
  - `read`: 73
  - `edit`: 18
  - `write`: 11

Final useful output was basically:

- 3 unique test templates in `ets-templates`
- mirrored into `ets-cts-7.0`

Final files:
- `recursive_type_alias_rhs_contexts_n.*`
- `type_alias_generic_class_constraint_n.*`
- `type_alias_type_argument_arity_n.*`

## Why it took extra prompts/tool calls

Main causes of rework:

1. **Initial prompt mixed conflicting goals**
   - spec-conformant tests
   - implementation-difference tests
   - crash tests
   - characterization of current behavior

   Later you clarified:
   - no characterization tests
   - only spec-conformant tests

   That forced rewrites/removals.

2. **Naming rules came late**
   - you later had to say: use spec feature/sub-feature names, not regression/difference/impl wording

3. **Seed test came late**
   - `32651.ets` was introduced after a lot of work had already started

4. **Deduplication happened late**
   - the agent added files, then later audited existing tests and removed/trimmed duplicates
   - one whole positive file got removed after the fact

5. **Release mirroring came late**
   - copying to `../ets-cts-7.0` became a separate phase instead of part of the original deliverable

6. **The agent worked in many small batches**
   - ~100 assistant tool-calling turns, average under 2 tools/turn
   - that usually means too much incremental search/read/write instead of one batched discovery pass

## How you could get the same result faster

### Best improvement: state all non-negotiables up front

If your real target was:

- spec-conformant only
- no crash/characterization tests
- dedupe against existing tests first
- use `32651.ets` as seed only
- mirror final result to `ets-cts-7.0`
- use spec-style names
- parameterize where appropriate

…then saying that in the first prompt would likely have cut this from **12 prompts to 1–2**.

### Also ask for this workflow explicitly

Tell the agent to:

1. batch-search implementation/spec/existing tests
2. propose final unique test set
3. only then write files
4. mirror to release dir
5. validate

That would reduce a lot of `bash/read/edit/write` churn.

## A better one-shot prompt

Use something like this next time:

> Use the canonical `PI_RESOURCE_*` path hints already loaded in session.  
> Task: for the ArkTS feature **Type Aliases**, find the frontend implementation and relevant spec sections, then add **only spec-conformant** CTS tests.  
>  
> Hard requirements:
> - use frontend test `32651.ets` only as a seed/reference; **do not modify frontend files**
> - write tests in `ets-templates` and mirror the final deduped result into `../ets-cts-7.0`
> - **no characterization tests**, **no crash-only tests**, **no “current behavior” capture**
> - names must follow **spec feature/sub-feature** wording; use `_n` for negative tests
> - before writing, audit existing type-alias tests and **skip/remove duplicates or near-duplicates**
> - parameterize where natural
> - minimize tool calls: do one batched discovery pass before writing
> - final report must list: implementation files, spec refs, unique tests added, duplicates skipped, validation results, and touched files
>  
> Workflow:
> 1. find implementation + spec refs  
> 2. audit existing tests in both suites  
> 3. decide the final unique test set  
> 4. write only that final set  
> 5. mirror to `ets-cts-7.0`  
> 6. validate with `es2panda` if practical  
>  
> Do not stop to ask intermediate questions unless blocked.

## If you want a safer 2-prompt version

Prompt 1:
- “Do steps 1–3 only. No writes yet. Show exact final files/cases and duplicates to skip.”

Prompt 2:
- “Approved. Execute exactly that plan.”

That often reduces total tool calls because it avoids write/delete/rename churn.

---

## Best pi idea: a reusable prompt template first

For your workflow, the **highest ROI** is probably a **prompt template** first, then later a skill/extension if you like it.

### Template idea
Create:

`.pi/prompts/arkts-spec-port.md`

```md
---
description: Spec-first ArkTS CTS authoring from frontend/spec with dedupe and release mirroring
argument-hint: "<feature> [frontend-test-id]"
---
Use the canonical PI_RESOURCE_* path hints already loaded in session.

Task: for feature "$1", create or refine ArkTS CTS tests in `ets-templates` and mirror the final unique result into `../ets-cts-7.0`.

If provided, use frontend test "$2" only as a seed/reference. Do not modify frontend files.

Hard requirements:
- spec-conformant only
- no characterization tests
- no crash-only tests
- audit existing tests first and skip near-duplicates
- use spec feature/sub-feature naming
- `_n` for negative tests
- parameterize where natural
- minimize tool calls by batching search/read before writing

Workflow:
1. Find frontend implementation and relevant spec sections.
2. Audit existing tests in both suites.
3. Decide the final unique test set.
4. Write only the final set.
5. Mirror to `../ets-cts-7.0`.
6. Validate if practical.
7. End with changed files, skipped duplicates, validation, and uncertainties.
```

Usage:

```bash
/arkts-spec-port "Type Aliases" 32651
```

## If you want something more powerful

### Skill idea
`arkts-spec-port`

Why a skill fits:
- this is a repeatable repo-specific workflow
- skills are good for checklists, helper scripts, and reference docs
- you can force it with `/skill:arkts-spec-port`

The skill would encode:
- env-path usage
- spec-first policy
- dedupe-before-write
- mirror-to-release rule
- naming rules
- validation checklist

### Extension idea
`arkts-port-guard`

A project-local extension could:

- register `/arkts-port <feature> [seed]`
- block `edit/write` until:
  - spec refs found
  - existing tests scanned
  - dedupe checklist completed
- show a status widget:
  - discover
  - dedupe
  - write
  - mirror
  - validate

That would directly cut rework.

## My recommendation

In order:

1. **Add the prompt template**
2. If it proves useful, convert it into a **skill**
3. Only build an **extension** if you want enforcement/automation

If you want, I can next draft either:
- the actual `.pi/prompts/arkts-spec-port.md`, or
- a `.pi/skills/arkts-spec-port/SKILL.md` version.
analyze previous session and tell how I could achieve the same result faster: (in less prompts or tool calls). Next propose idea for pi-agent extension, skill or template-prompt

analyze previous session and tell how I could achieve the same result (new, non-duplicated Spec-conformant ets-templates tests that 1) cover some language feature 2) discover non-conformant language implementation in frontend/runtime 3) possibly find tests that crash frontend/runtime)  faster (in less prompts or tool calls). Next propose idea for pi-agent extension, skill or template-prompt

/home/mbolshov/runtime_work/static_core/plugins/ets/tests/stdlib-templates/readme.md:17:For generating tests against ArkTS stdlib the Python library Jinja2 is used. For detail information about Jinja2 template, see :<https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/en/3.1.x/>
/home/mbolshov/runtime_work/static_core/plugins/ets/tests/stdlib-templates/readme.md:18:The common structure of a template is:
/home/mbolshov/runtime_work/static_core/plugins/ets/tests/stdlib-templates/readme.md:20:- The template itself. It contains a mix of Jinja2 statements and ArkTS code. The content will be rendered into clear ArkTS source code. 
/home/mbolshov/runtime_work/static_core/plugins/ets/tests/stdlib-templates/readme.md:21:- The configuration file in the yaml format is a set of objects which will be used for rendering Jinja2 template. 
/home/mbolshov/runtime_work/static_core/plugins/ets/tests/stdlib-templates/readme.md:23:There are two levels of templates:
/home/mbolshov/runtime_work/static_core/plugins/ets/tests/stdlib-templates/readme.md:26:  - specific level that usually is constructed from common level templates via **include** command of Jinja2. This level can contain additional code for the task.
/home/mbolshov/runtime_work/static_core/plugins/ets/tests/stdlib-templates/readme.md:29:The specific level template contains the loop over all objects in the configuration file. Then the generator takes an object from the configuration file and replaces corresponded variables in the template. Separated test object will be generated for each object. So number of tests created by generator will be equal number of objects in the configuration file.

---
cases:
    - desc: primitive type argument violates a primitive constraint
      decl: |-
          class C<T extends string> {}

          type A = C<int>
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0228: Type argument 'Int' should be a subtype of 'String' constraint"

    - desc: boolean type argument violates a primitive constraint
      decl: |-
          class C<T extends string> {}

          type A = C<boolean>
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0228: Type argument 'Boolean' should be a subtype of 'String' constraint"

    - desc: class type argument violates a class constraint
      decl: |-
          class Base {}
          class Other {}
          class C<T extends Base> {}

          type A = C<Other>
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0228: Type argument 'Other' should be a subtype of 'Base' constraint"

    - desc: class type argument violates an interface constraint
      decl: |-
          interface I {}
          class Other {}
          class C<T extends I> {}

          type A = C<Other>
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0228: Type argument 'Other' should be a subtype of 'I' constraint"

---
cases:
    - desc: tuple-element recursive reference
      defs: |-
          type TupleRec = [TupleRec | undefined]
      use: |-
          let t: TupleRec = [undefined]
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0311: Circular type alias reference"

    - desc: union-member function recursive reference
      defs: |-
          type UnionFuncRec = string | (() => UnionFuncRec)
      use: |-
          let f: UnionFuncRec = (): UnionFuncRec => 'ok'
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0311: Circular type alias reference"

# Copyright (c) 2026 Huawei Device Co., Ltd.

#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.

---
cases:
    - desc: single-parameter generic alias rejects too many type arguments
      defs: |-
          type PathTooMany<T> = T[]
      use: |-
          let x: PathTooMany<int, string> = []
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0274: Expected at least 1 type arguments, but got 2."

    - desc: two-parameter generic alias rejects too many type arguments
      defs: |-
          type PairArity<T, U> = [T, U]
      use: |-
          let x: PairArity<int, string, boolean> = [1, 'a']
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0274: Expected at least 2 type arguments, but got 3."

set -e

export STATIC_ROOT_DIR="/home/mbolshov/runtime_work/static_core"

generator-class: runner.extensions.generators.ets_cts.ets_templates_generator.EtsTemplatesGenerator

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -- coding: utf-8 --
#
# Copyright (c) 2024-2026 Huawei Device Co., Ltd.
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
# http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#

import re
from pathlib import Path

from runner import common_exceptions
from runner.extensions.generators.ets_cts.params import Params
from runner.extensions.generators.ets_cts.template import Template
from runner.logger import Log
from runner.utils import strip_test_name, write_2_file

_LOGGER = Log.get_logger(__file__)

FILE_NAME = "file_name"
FILE_SUFFIX = "test_suffix"


class Benchmark:
    def __init__(self, test_path: Path, output: Path, test_full_name: str, ets_templates_path: Path,
                 out_extension: str) -> None:
        self.__input = test_path
        self.__output = output.parent
        self.__name = test_path.name
        self.__out_extension = out_extension
        self.__full_name = Path(test_full_name).with_suffix('').name
        self.__ets_templates_path = ets_templates_path

    @property
    def out_extension(self) -> str:
        return self.__out_extension

    @out_extension.setter
    def out_extension(self, value: str) -> None:
        self.__out_extension = value

    @staticmethod
    def get_name_suffix(test_content: str) -> tuple[str | None, str | None]:
        name_pattern = rf'{FILE_NAME}:\s*([\'"])(.*?)\1'
        suffix_pattern = rf'{FILE_SUFFIX}:\s*([\'"])(.*?)\1'
        test_lines = test_content.split('\n')
        file_name = None
        suffix = None
        for line in test_lines:
            suffix_pattern_match = re.search(suffix_pattern, line)
            name_pattern_match = re.search(name_pattern, line)
            if suffix_pattern_match:
                suffix = suffix_pattern_match.group(2)
            if name_pattern_match:
                file_name = name_pattern_match.group(2)
        return file_name if file_name else None, suffix if suffix else None

    def generate(self) -> list[str]:
        _LOGGER.all(f"Generating test: {self.__name}")
        name_without_ext, file_ext = strip_test_name(self.__input)
        self.out_extension = file_ext
        params = Params(self.__input, name_without_ext).generate()
        template = Template(self.__input, params, self.__ets_templates_path)

        _LOGGER.all(f"Starting generate test template: {self.__name}")
        rendered_tests = template.render_template()
        if len(rendered_tests) <= 0:
            raise common_exceptions.TestNotExistException(f"{self.__name}: Incorrect test template")

        tests = self._write_tests_to_file(rendered_tests, name_without_ext, template)

        _LOGGER.all(f"Finish generating test template for: {self.__name}")
        return tests

    def _write_tests_to_file(self, rendered_tests: list[str], name_without_ext: str, template: Template) -> list[str]:

        tests = []
        for i, test in enumerate(rendered_tests):
            file_name, suffix = Benchmark.get_name_suffix(test)
            test_suffix = suffix if suffix is not None else i
            file_name = file_name if file_name else name_without_ext
            name = f"{file_name}_{test_suffix}" if len(rendered_tests) > 1 else file_name
            full_name = f"{self.__full_name}_{test_suffix}" if len(rendered_tests) > 1 else self.__full_name
            test_content = template.generate_test(test, full_name)
            file_path = self.__output / f"{name}{self.out_extension}"
            write_2_file(file_path=file_path, content=test_content, disallow_overwrite=True)
            tests.append(str(file_path))

        return tests

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -- coding: utf-8 --
#
# Copyright (c) 2021-2026 Huawei Device Co., Ltd.
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
# http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#

import os
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path

import yaml
from jinja2 import Environment, FileSystemLoader, TemplateSyntaxError, select_autoescape

from runner.extensions.generators.ets_cts.yaml_extension import YamlExtension
from runner.sts_utils.constants import (
    META_COPYRIGHT,
    META_END_COMMENT,
    META_END_STRING,
    META_START_COMMENT,
    META_START_STRING,
)
from runner.sts_utils.exceptions import InvalidFileFormatException, InvalidMetaException, UnknownTemplateException
from runner.utils import read_file, remove_template_copyright

ROOT_PATH = Path(os.path.realpath(os.path.dirname(__file__)))
BENCH_PATH = ROOT_PATH / "test_template.tpl"
COPYRIGHT_PATH = ROOT_PATH / "copyright.txt"


@dataclass
class Meta:
    config: dict[str, str]
    code: str


class Template:
    def __init__(self, test_path: Path, params: dict[str, list], ets_templates_path: Path) -> None:
        self.test_path = str(test_path)
        self.text = read_file(test_path)
        self.__params = params
        self.__jinja_env = Environment(
            loader=FileSystemLoader([test_path.parent.as_posix(), ets_templates_path.as_posix()]),
            autoescape=select_autoescape(),
            extensions=[YamlExtension],
        )
        self.__jinja_env.__setattr__('current_test_path_dir', test_path.parent)

        if self.is_copyright:
            self.__copyright = read_file(COPYRIGHT_PATH)
        else:
            self.__copyright, out_content = self.__get_in_out_content(self.text, META_START_COMMENT, META_END_COMMENT)
            self.text = out_content.strip()

    @property
    def is_copyright(self) -> bool:
        return META_COPYRIGHT not in self.text

    @staticmethod
    def __replace_symbols(text: str) -> str:
        codes = [(''', "'"), ('"', '"'), ('>', '>'), ('<', '<'), ('&', '&')]
        for old, new in codes:
            text = text.replace(old, new)
        return text

    def render_template(self) -> list[str]:
        try:
            template = self.__jinja_env.from_string(self.text)
            rendered = template.render(**self.__params).strip()
        except TemplateSyntaxError as exc:
            raise InvalidFileFormatException(message=f"Template Syntax Error: {exc.message}",
                                             filepath=self.test_path) from exc
        except Exception as exc:
            raise UnknownTemplateException(filepath=self.test_path, exception=exc) from exc

        meta_start = META_START_STRING if self.is_copyright else META_START_COMMENT
        return [meta_start + e for e in rendered.split(meta_start) if e]

    def generate_test(self, test: str, fullname: str) -> str:
        meta = self.__get_meta(test)
        meta.config["name"] = fullname
        yaml_content = yaml.dump(meta.config)

        bench_template = read_file(BENCH_PATH)
        bench_template = remove_template_copyright(bench_template)
        bench_code = bench_template.format(
            copyright=self.__copyright.strip(),
            description=yaml_content.strip(),
            code=meta.code.strip())
        return bench_code

    def __get_meta(self, text: str) -> Meta:
        test_text = self.__replace_symbols(text)
        inside_content, outside_content = self.__get_in_out_content(test_text, META_START_STRING, META_END_STRING)
        return Meta(config=self.__parse_yaml(inside_content), code=outside_content)

    def __parse_yaml(self, text: str) -> dict[str, str]:
        try:
            result: dict[str, str] = yaml.safe_load(text)
        except Exception as exc:
            raise InvalidFileFormatException(message=f"Could not load YAML in test params: {exc!s}",
                                             filepath=self.test_path) from exc
        return result

    def __get_in_out_content(self, text: str, meta_start: str, meta_end: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
        start, end = text.find(meta_start), text.find(meta_end)
        if start > end or start == -1 or end == -1:
            raise InvalidMetaException(f"{Path(self.test_path).name}: Invalid meta or meta does not exist")
        inside_content = text[start + len(meta_start):end]
        outside_content = text[:start] + text[end + len(meta_end):]
        return inside_content, outside_content

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -- coding: utf-8 --
#
# Copyright (c) 2026 Huawei Device Co., Ltd.
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
# http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#

from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

from jinja2 import Environment
from jinja2.ext import Extension
from jinja2.nodes import Assign
from jinja2.parser import Parser

from runner.sts_utils.test_parameters import parse_yaml


class YamlExtension(Extension):

    def __init__(self, environment: Environment):
        super().__init__(environment)
        self.tags.add("yaml")

    def parse(self, parser: Parser) -> Assign:
        lineno = next(parser.stream).lineno
        template = parser.parse_expression()
        parser.stream.expect("name:as")
        assign_node = Assign(lineno=lineno)
        assign_node.target = parser.parse_assign_target(name_only=True)
        assign_node.node = self.call_method("call_read_yaml", [template], lineno=lineno)
        return assign_node

    def call_read_yaml(self, name: str) -> Any:  # type: ignore[explicit-any]
        yaml_dir = self.environment.getattr(self.environment, 'current_test_path_dir')
        return parse_yaml(str(Path(yaml_dir, name)))

.. index::
   alias
   anonymous type
   name
   type alias
   name
   generic type

Type aliases can be recursively referenced inside the right-hand side of a type
alias declaration.

In a type alias defined as ``type A = something``, *A* can be used recursively
if it is one of the following:

-  Array element type: ``type A = A[]``; or
-  Type argument of a generic type: ``type A = C<A>``.

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    type A = A[] // OK, used as element type

    class C<T> { /*body*/}
    type B = C<B> // OK, used as a type argument

    type D = string | Array<D> // OK

Any other use including unresolvable circular references causes a
:index:`compile-time error`, because the compiler does not have enough
information about the defined alias:

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    type E = E          // Compile-time error
    type F = string | F // Compile-time error
    type C<T> = T
    type A = C<A>       // Compile-time error


.. index::
   type alias
   alias
   recursive reference
   type alias declaration
   array element
   type argument
   generic type
   compiler

The same rules apply to a generic type alias defined as
``type A<T> = something``:

.. code-block-meta:
   expect-cte:

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    type A<T> = Array<A<T>> // OK, A<T> is used as a type argument
    type A<T> = string | Array<A<T>> // OK

    type A<T> = A<T> // Compile-time error

A :index:`compile-time error` occurs if a generic type alias is used without
a type argument:

.. code-block-meta:
   expect-cte:

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    type A<T> = Array<A> // Compile-time error

.. note::
   There is no restriction on using a type parameter *T* in the right side of
   a type alias declaration. The following code is valid:

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    type NodeValue<T> = T | Array<T> | Array<NodeValue<T>>;

.. index::
   alias
   generic type
   type argument
   type alias
   type parameter

A type parameter of a type alias can *depend* on another type parameter
of the same generic.

.. index::
   type parameter
   generic

.. code-block:: typescript

Type Parameter Constraint
=========================

.. meta:
    frontend_status: Done

If possible instantiations need to be constrained, then an individual
*constraint* can be set for each type parameter after the keyword ``extends``.
A constraint can have the form of any type.

If no constraint is specified,
then the constraint is :ref:`Type Any`, i.e., the lacking explicit constraint
effectively means ``extends Any``. As a consequence, the type parameter is not
compatible with :ref:`Type Object`, and has neither methods nor fields available
for use.

If type parameter *T* has type constraint *S*, then the actual type of the
generic instantiation must be a subtype of *S* (see :ref:`Subtyping`). If the
constraint *S* is a non-nullish type (see :ref:`Nullish Types`), then *T* is
also non-nullish.

.. index::
   constraint
   instantiation
   type parameter
   extends keyword
   type reference
   union type normalization
   object
   compatibility
   assignability
   nullish-type
   non-nullish-type
   any type
   type argument
   generic instantiation
   instantiation
   subtyping
   subtype


.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    class Base {}
    class Derived extends Base { }
    class SomeType { }

    class G<T extends Base> { }

    let x = new G<Base>      // OK
    let y = new G<Derived>   // OK
    let z = new G<SomeType>  // Compile-time : SomeType is not compatible with Base

    class H<T extends Base|SomeType> {}
    let h1 = new H<Base>     // OK
    let h2 = new H<Derived>  // OK
    let h3 = new H<SomeType> // OK
    let h4 = new H<Object>   // Compile-time : Object is not compatible with Base|SomeType

    class Exotic<T extends "aa"| "bb"> {}
    let e1 = new Exotic<"aa">   // OK
    let e2 = new Exotic<"cc">  // Compile-time : "cc" is not compatible with "aa"| "bb"

    class A {
      f1: number = 0
      f2: string = ""
      f3: boolean = false
    }
    class B <T extends keyof A> {}
    let b1 = new B<'f1'>    // OK
    let b2 = new B<'f0'>    // Compile-time error as 'f0' does not fit the constraint
    let b3 = new B<keyof A> // OK

A type parameter of a generic can *depend* on an earlier type parameter
of the same generic.

.. index::
   type parameter
   generic

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    class G<T, S extends T> {}

    class Base {}
    class Derived extends Base { }
    class SomeType { }

    type A<T> = string | Array<A<T>> // OK

    type A<T> = A<T> // Compile-time error

A :index:`compile-time error` occurs if a generic type alias is used without
a type argument:

.. code-block-meta:
   expect-cte:

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    type A<T> = Array<A> // Compile-time error

.. note::
   There is no restriction on using a type parameter *T* in the right side of
   a type alias declaration. The following code is valid:

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    type NodeValue<T> = T | Array<T> | Array<NodeValue<T>>;

.. index::

A type parameter of a type alias can *depend* on another type parameter
of the same generic.

.. index::

.. code-block:: typescript
   :linenos:

    type X<T, S extends T> = T | S

A :index:`compile-time error` occurs if type arguments are provided for
non-generic class, interface, type alias, method, or function.

In the explicit generic instantiation *G* <``T``:sub:`1`, ``...``, ``T``:sub:`n`>,
*G* is the generic declaration, and  <``T``:sub:`1`, ``...``, ``T``:sub:`n`> is
the list of its type arguments.

..
   lines 312, 314, 336 - initially the type was *T*:sub:`1`, ``...``, *T*:sub:`n`
   lines 321, 322 - initially *C*:sub:`1`, ``...``, *C*:sub:`n` and *T*:sub:`1`, ``...``, *T*:sub:`n`

If type parameters *T*:sub:`1`, ``...``, *T*:sub:`n` of a generic
declaration are constrained by the corresponding ``C``:sub:`1`, ``...``,
``C``:sub:`n`, then *T*:sub:`i` is assignable to each constraint type
*C*:sub:`i` (see :ref:`Assignability`). All subtypes of the type listed
in the corresponding constraint have each type argument *T*:sub:`i` of the
parameterized declaration ranging over them.

A generic instantiation *G* <``T``:sub:`1`, ``...``, ``T``:sub:`n`> is
*well-formed* if **all** of the following is true:

-  The generic declaration name is *G*;
-  The number of type arguments equals the number of type parameters of *G*; and
-  All type arguments are assignable to the corresponding type parameter
   constraint (see :ref:`Assignability`).

A :index:`compile-time error` occurs if an instantiation is not well-formed.

Unless explicitly stated otherwise in appropriate sections, this specification
discusses generic versions of class type, interface type, or function.

Any two generic instantiations are considered *provably distinct* if:

-  Both are parameterizations of distinct generic declarations; or
-  Any of their type arguments is provably distinct.

.. index::

from pathlib import Path

from runner.sts_utils.test_parameters import parse_yaml

YAML_EXTENSIONS = ".yaml"
PARAM_SUFFIX = ".params"


class Params:
    def __init__(self, input_path: Path, bench_name: str) -> None:
        param_name = f"{bench_name}{PARAM_SUFFIX}{YAML_EXTENSIONS}"
        self.__param_path = input_path.parent / param_name

    def generate(self) -> dict[str, list]:
        if not self.__param_path.exists():
            return {}
        params: dict[str, list] = parse_yaml(str(self.__param_path))
        return params

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
#
# Copyright (c) 2024-2025 Huawei Device Co., Ltd.
# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
# You may obtain a copy of the License at
#
# http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
#
# Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
# limitations under the License.
#

# This file provides functions thar are responsible for loading test parameters
# that are used in templates (yaml lists).

import os.path as ospath
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any

import yaml

from runner.sts_utils.constants import LIST_PREFIX, YAML_EXTENSIONS
from runner.sts_utils.exceptions import InvalidFileFormatException
from runner.utils import iter_files, read_file

Params = dict


def load_params(dirpath: Path) -> Params:
    """
    Loads all parameters for a directory
    """
    result = {}

    for filename, filepath in iter_files(dirpath, allowed_ext=YAML_EXTENSIONS):
        name_without_ext, _ = ospath.splitext(filename)
        if not name_without_ext.startswith(LIST_PREFIX):
            raise InvalidFileFormatException(message="Lists of parameters must start with 'list.'", filepath=filepath)
        listname = name_without_ext[len(LIST_PREFIX):]
        params: list[dict[str, Any]] = parse_yaml(filepath)  # type: ignore[explicit-any]
        if not isinstance(params, list):
            raise InvalidFileFormatException(message="Parameters list must be YAML array", filepath=filepath)
        result[listname] = params
    return result


def parse_yaml(path: str) -> Any:       # type: ignore[explicit-any]
    """
    Parses a single YAML list of parameters
    """
    text = read_file(path)
    try:
        params = yaml.safe_load(text)
    # NOTE(pronai): can we make it more precise?
    except Exception as common_exp:
        raise InvalidFileFormatException(
            message=f"Could not load YAML: {common_exp!s}",
            filepath=path) from common_exp
    return params

content.text:
- defs: |-
          type E = E

    - defs: |-
          type E = string | E

    - defs: |-
          type A<T> = Array<A>

    - defs: |-
          type A<T> = A<T>

    - defs: |-
          type A = C
          type B = A
          type C = B

    # recursive cases
    - defs: |-
          type RU = number | string | RU // TypeError: Circular type alias reference
      use: |-
          let v1: RU = 1.0
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0311: Circular type alias reference"

    - defs: |-
          type RF = (p: RF) => RF
      use: |-
          let v1: RF = (p: RF): RF => { return p }

    - defs: |-
        { class ClassNestedInBlock{} }
        type TAlias = ClassNestedInBlock;

    - defs: |-
          enum Axis { X, Y, Z };
          type Fields = Axis[];
      use: |-
          let v: Fields = new Axis[6];
          let ok = v.length == 6;

    - defs: |-
        type TStringAlias? = String | undefined

    - defs: |-
        @interface ClassAuthor {}
        type A = ClassAuthor

    - defs: |-
        @interface ClassAuthor {}
        type A = ClassAuthor | String

    - defs: |-
        type A = class MyClassDec {}

    - defs: |-
        class AliasMe {}
        type Alias = AliasMe<X> // CTE:  TypeError: Type 'AliasMe' is not generic.

    - defs: |-
        class AliasMe<X> {}
        type Alias = AliasMe<X> // CTE: TypeError: Cannot find type 'X'.

    - defs: |-
        type A<T, U> = T | A<U, T>[]
      use: |-
        let xx: A<Boolean, String> = new A<Boolean, String>()  //CTE: TypeError: The union type is not constructible.
      expected_out: "Semantic error ESE0300: The union type is not constructible."

    - defs: |-
        final class FinitaLaComedia {}

    alias
tags: [compile-only, negative]

type A<T> = A<T>

content.text:
I can’t inspect the actual chat transcript of the previous session, but I can infer a lot from the repo/workdir artifacts.

### What the previous session appears to have achieved

Most recent activity is around:

`plugins/ets/tests/ets-templates/04.names_declarations_and_scopes/05.type_declarations/01.type_alias_declaration/`

Recent new template families there:

- `type_alias_generic_class_constraint_n.{ets,params.yaml}`
- `recursive_type_alias_rhs_contexts_n.{ets,params.yaml}`
- `type_alias_type_argument_arity_n.{ets,params.yaml}`

From the generated urunner reports I can confirm at least this result:

- `recursive_type_alias_rhs_contexts_n_0.ets`
- `recursive_type_alias_rhs_contexts_n_1.ets`

both **fail as new tests** because `es2panda` returns `0` and accepts code that the test expects to be rejected.

The logs show:

- expected: compile-time error `ESE0311: Circular type alias reference`
- actual: compiler succeeds

So the session successfully produced **new spec-driven negative tests** that exposed a **frontend non-conformance**.

This lines up with spec text in:

- `plugins/ets/doc/spec/4_names.rst`
  - recursive type alias use is only allowed in limited RHS contexts
  - “Any other use … causes a compile-time error”
- `plugins/ets/doc/spec/5_generics.rst`
  - well-formed instantiation requires correct type-argument count
  - type arguments must satisfy constraints

### One concrete inefficiency I can already see

Your current generated workdir suggests a missed cheap sanity-check:

- `type_alias_type_argument_arity_n.params.yaml` contains **2 cases**
- local template rendering also produces **2 rendered tests**
- but in `urunner-work/.../gen/` I only see a single generated file:
  - `type_alias_type_argument_arity_n.ets`

That suggests stale/incomplete generation or that validation stopped before you checked the fully rendered set.

A quick local render sanity check would have caught that in seconds.

---

## How to get the same result faster next time

### 1. Start from a **hotspot + crisp rule**, not broad exploration

Fastest path is:

1. grep **known failures / nearby failures**
2. pick a **tight spec rule**
3. search only the matching ETS template subtree

For this repo, that worked well because type-alias handling is already a “buggy neighborhood”:
- existing known failures exist in the same area
- the spec rule is sharp and easy to translate into positive/negative cases

So instead of:
- “find some interesting language feature”

use:
- “find an uncovered edge in a spec area that already has nearby failures”

That alone cuts a lot of prompts/tool calls.

---

### 2. Ask for the whole workflow in one prompt

Instead of interactive back-and-forth, use one prompt that says:

- spec section to read
- exact test subtree to search
- output format
- validation strategy
- stop condition

Example:

> Read only the relevant spec subsection under `$PI_LANG_SPEC` and the nearest existing tests under `$PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO/plugins/ets/tests/ets-templates/...`.  
> Find 3-6 uncovered, non-duplicated spec edges.  
> Prefer one parametric `.ets + .params.yaml` template over many standalone files.  
> For compile-only negative tests, validate with direct `es2panda` first, and run urunner only for finalists that show new behavior.  
> Return: spec citation, duplicate check, patch, exact repro commands, and whether each case is pass / spec mismatch / crash.

That usually avoids several clarification prompts.

---

### 3. Use **one parametric template** per rule cluster

For this kind of work, don’t create many tiny files first.

Better:

- 1 template
- 1 params yaml
- 4–8 cases around the same rule

Why faster:
- one duplicate search
- one write/edit cycle
- one render check
- one compile loop

Your recent session already moved in this direction; I’d push it even harder.

---

### 4. For compile-only negative tests, **skip full urunner first**

This is the biggest speedup.

For tests tagged like:

- `compile-only`
- `negative`

you usually do **not** need full urunner initially.

Cheaper flow:

1. render template locally
2. compile generated `.ets` files directly with `es2panda`
3. only if behavior is interesting/new, run targeted urunner for report-quality confirmation

That is much faster than jumping straight to:

`runner.sh panda-int ets-cts ...`

Use urunner only for:
- final confirmation
- standardized logs
- report artifacts
- dependency-heavy cases

---

### 5. Add a **render sanity check** before running anything

Before validation, always answer:

- how many concrete tests will this template generate?
- what are their names?
- did suffix splitting work?
- are any cases silently missing?

A tiny local Python render check is enough.

This would likely have saved time in your `type_alias_type_argument_arity_n` case.

---

### 6. Search duplicates by **spec rule + error code + shape**

Don’t just grep by file name.

For negative spec tests, fast duplicate screening is:

- same subtree
- same expected error code
- same syntactic shape
- same semantic rule

For your recent work, good duplicate queries would have been:

- `ESE0311` + type alias subtree
- `ESE0228` + generic constraint + alias/class
- `ESE0274` + type argument count

That is enough to reject most duplicates quickly without many file reads.

---

### 7. Use a **2-phase generation strategy**

#### Phase A: bug-finding
- write only the best 1–2 templates
- render
- direct compile
- keep only cases that find a mismatch / crash

#### Phase B: suite-quality polish
- improve descriptions
- normalize wording
- add more cases only if needed
- urunner confirmation

This avoids polishing tests that don’t reveal anything.

---

## Minimal fast workflow I’d use next time

You can usually do this in about **6 tool steps**:

1. **Parallel read/search**
   - relevant spec lines
   - nearby tests
   - known/new reports in same area
2. **Gap table**
   - 3–6 candidate cases
3. **One write/edit**
   - parametric template + params
4. **Local render check**
   - verify generated case count/names
5. **Direct compile loop**
   - `es2panda` on generated cases
6. **One targeted urunner run**
   - only for interesting finalists

That is likely much fewer prompts/tool calls than a conversational explore→write→run→adjust loop.

---

## Best prompt template for this task

Use something like this next time:

> Goal: add new, non-duplicated, spec-conformant ets-templates tests that cover one language feature and ideally reveal frontend/runtime non-conformance or crashes.  
>  
> Use these paths:  
> - runtime repo: `$PI_RESOURCE_RUNTIME_REPO`  
> - build dir: `$PI_BUILD_DIR`  
> - generated tests dir: `$PI_TEST_DIR`  
> - spec dir: `$PI_LANG_SPEC`  
>  
> Workflow:  
> 1. Read only the exact spec subsection relevant to one narrow feature.  
> 2. Search the nearest ets-templates subtree for existing coverage and duplicates.  
> 3. Search urunner known/new reports for nearby failure hotspots.  
> 4. Propose a compact coverage-gap table with 3–6 candidate tests.  
> 5. Implement only the best 1–2 candidates, preferably as one parametric template.  
> 6. Dry-render the template locally and verify generated case count/names.  
> 7. For compile-only negative tests, validate with direct `es2panda` before urunner.  
> 8. Run targeted urunner only for cases that show new behavior.  
> 9. Return patch, spec citations, non-duplication rationale, and exact repro commands.  
>  
> Avoid broad repo exploration and avoid full-suite runs unless required.

---

## Best pi-agent idea: a skill

### Skill name
`ets-templates-spec-gap-hunter`

### What it should do
Given:
- spec section or keyword
- ETS template subtree
- mode: `compile-only` / `runtime` / `crash-hunt`
- max new tests

it should automatically:

1. read the spec section
2. extract normative rules/examples
3. search nearby tests for coverage
4. search known/new urunner reports for hotspots
5. score candidate uncovered cases
6. generate a parametric `.ets + .params.yaml`
7. dry-render it
8. run minimal validation:
   - direct `es2panda` for compile-only
   - minimal `ark`/urunner for runtime
9. classify results:
   - duplicate
   - conformant
   - new non-conformance
   - crash
10. emit:
   - patch
   - repro commands
   - short gap report mapping spec rule → new test

### Why this would help
It compresses the whole loop from many prompts into one invocation, and it targets exactly the pain points visible in your session:

- too much manual duplicate checking
- full-run validation too early
- missing render sanity checks
- not exploiting nearby failure hotspots enough

---

If you want, I can also turn this into:
1. a **ready-to-paste one-shot prompt** specifically for the `type_alias_declaration` area, or  
2. a **skill spec** in markdown for pi-agent.
